findings.summary: get_testing_config declares return type TestingConfig but builds and returns Dict[str, Any] suppressed with type: ignore
evidence: Line 446: result: Dict[str, Any] = {}
Line 510: return result  # type: ignore[return-value]  # dict matches TestingConfig shape
TestingConfig is a TypedDict but the returned dict may be missing keys that TestingConfig expects, with no runtime validation
findings.suggestion: Either return a properly constructed TestingConfig (fill all keys before returning) or return ResolvedTestingConfig which already captures defaults as a frozen dataclass

findings.summary: ResolvedTestingConfig dataclass is defined but never exported, never instantiated, and never used anywhere in the codebase
evidence: ResolvedTestingConfig defined at line 72 of config_loader.py
Absent from infrastructure/core/__init__.py exports and __all__
Grep across all .py files finds only its definition site — no usages
findings.suggestion: Either use ResolvedTestingConfig as the return type of get_testing_config (replacing the Dict[str, Any] construction) or delete it

findings.summary: get_config_as_dict returns Dict[str, str] (flat stringly-typed map) despite ManuscriptConfig TypedDict schema being available above it
evidence: load_config() returns Optional[ManuscriptConfig] — a typed nested schema
get_config_as_dict() immediately flattens this to Dict[str, str] with string keys like PROJECT_TITLE, AUTHOR_NAME
Callers lose all type information; downstream code accesses config by magic string keys
findings.suggestion: Return ManuscriptConfig (or a typed projection dataclass) from the primary config accessor; keep the flat dict as a separate env-var serialization helper

findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py mixes re-export aggregator and implementation module roles, creating confusion about where logic lives
evidence: Lines 24-47: core.py re-imports and re-exports everything from citations, metadata, models, platforms with # noqa: F401
Lines 51-333: core.py also directly implements create_publication_package, create_submission_checklist, generate_doi_badge, create_publication_announcement, validate_publication_readiness
publishing/__init__.py then imports from .core, creating a chain: __init__ -> core -> citations/metadata/models/platforms
findings.suggestion: Split core.py: move the re-export aggregation into __init__.py directly, and keep core.py as a pure implementation module for the functions it actually defines

findings.summary: RenderManager directly instantiates all four concrete renderer classes in __init__, with no dependency injection or abstraction boundary
evidence: Lines 30-33: self.pdf_renderer = PDFRenderer(self.config), self.slides_renderer = SlidesRenderer(self.config), self.web_renderer = WebRenderer(self.config), self.poster_renderer = PosterRenderer(self.config)
No Protocol or abstract base class for renderers
Cannot substitute a renderer without modifying RenderManager
findings.suggestion: Define a Renderer Protocol with a render() method and accept renderer instances as optional constructor parameters, defaulting to the concrete types

findings.summary: validate_publication_readiness returns Dict[str, Any] and requires type: ignore casts for list appends on typed keys
evidence: Lines 272-277: readiness dict built with heterogeneous value types (bool, int, list[str], list[str])
Lines 310, 315, 320, 325, 329, 331: all list appends carry # type: ignore comments
Return type Dict[str, Any] gives callers no information about the dict shape
findings.suggestion: Define a PublicationReadiness TypedDict or dataclass with typed fields ready_for_publication: bool, completeness_score: int, missing_elements: List[str], recommendations: List[str]

findings.summary: ollama>=0.6.1 is declared as a hard runtime dependency despite the LLM subsystem being entirely optional and guarded by requires_ollama markers
evidence: pyproject.toml line 49: "ollama>=0.6.1" in [project].dependencies (not in [dependency-groups])
All LLM tests use @pytest.mark.requires_ollama to skip when Ollama is unavailable
ollama is only imported in infrastructure/llm/ — an entire optional subsystem
findings.suggestion: Move ollama to an optional dependency group (e.g., [dependency-groups] llm = ["ollama>=0.6.1"]) and guard all imports with try/except ImportError

findings.summary: networkx>=3.0 is declared as a hard dependency justified by cognitive_case_diagrams but no infrastructure module imports it
evidence: pyproject.toml line 48: "networkx>=3.0" with comment "# Graph/network analysis (cognitive_case_diagrams)"
output/cognitive_case_diagrams/ is an output directory, not an infrastructure module
Grep across all infrastructure/**/*.py finds zero networkx imports
findings.suggestion: Remove networkx from core dependencies; add it to an optional dependency group or to the specific project's pyproject.toml if used in project scripts

findings.summary: plotly is declared as a hard runtime dependency but is only used via a lazy local import inside one function in dashboard_generator.py
evidence: pyproject.toml line 47: "plotly>=5.0.0" in [project].dependencies
infrastructure/reporting/dashboard_generator.py line 961: import plotly.graph_objects as go inside a function body
The lazy import pattern signals the author already anticipated optional use
findings.suggestion: Move plotly to an optional dependency group; the existing lazy import already provides a clean fallback point — add an ImportError handler there

findings.summary: get_review_types and ResolvedTestingConfig are defined in config_loader.py but not re-exported from infrastructure/core/__init__.py, breaking the stable public interface contract
evidence: __init__.py from .config_loader import block lists: find_config_file, get_config_as_dict, get_config_as_env_vars, get_translation_languages, load_config — get_review_types is absent
__all__ in __init__.py does not contain get_review_types
infrastructure/llm/review/pipeline_runner.py imports get_review_types directly from infrastructure.core.config_loader (submodule bypass)
findings.suggestion: Add get_review_types to the from .config_loader import block and to __all__ in infrastructure/core/__init__.py so all callers can use the stable public interface

findings.summary: Several test files import directly from submodule paths rather than the package public interface, coupling tests to implementation location
evidence: test_publishing_core.py line 9: from infrastructure.publishing.core import create_publication_package
test_publishing_edge_cases.py line 7: from infrastructure.publishing import core as publishing
The stable public interface is infrastructure.publishing (the __init__) — bypassing it means tests would not catch interface regressions
findings.suggestion: Import from infrastructure.publishing (the public __init__) in all test files; test the contract, not the implementation location

findings.summary: test_duplicate_headings_deduplicated uses result.count() with an else True fallback that makes the assertion always pass for the actual return type (set)
evidence: Line 115: assert result.count("introduction") == 1 if hasattr(result, "count") else True
extract_headings returns a set, which has no .count() method
hasattr(result, 'count') is False for a set, so the else True branch always executes — the uniqueness assertion never fires
findings.suggestion: Replace with: assert len([h for h in result if h == 'introduction']) == 1 or simply assert 'introduction' in result (sets already deduplicate, so a membership check suffices)

findings.summary: test_no_false_positives_in_code_blocks does not actually test the false-positive scenario it names
evidence: Lines 78-84: test comment acknowledges 'code block lines with # are tricky' but the test input contains no code block
The test only verifies that a real heading outside a code block is found — this is already covered by test_single_h1_heading
The actual defect (# inside a fenced code block being extracted as a heading) goes untested
findings.suggestion: Provide input containing a fenced code block with # lines and assert those lines are NOT in the result; mark the test as xfail if the function is known to have this limitation

findings.summary: pytest filterwarnings globally suppresses UserWarning and FutureWarning, potentially hiding real deprecation signals from dependencies
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 120-121: "ignore::UserWarning", "ignore::FutureWarning"
These are broad suppression patterns with no module scope restriction
Libraries like numpy, matplotlib, and plotly use FutureWarning for breaking API changes
findings.suggestion: Scope warning filters to specific modules that are known to emit spurious warnings (e.g., 'ignore::FutureWarning:matplotlib') rather than suppressing the entire category

findings.summary: conftest.py uses importlib.util.spec_from_file_location to import test helpers because the test directory structure mirrors infrastructure packages and causes sys.path shadows
evidence: Lines 32-37: importlib.util.spec_from_file_location used instead of a normal import for _test_helpers
Comment at line 22: 'Do NOT add tests/infra_tests to sys.path - it would shadow infrastructure.* imports'
tests/infra_tests/validation/ and tests/infra_tests/rendering/ share names with infrastructure/validation/ and infrastructure/rendering/
findings.suggestion: Rename test subdirectories to break the mirror symmetry (e.g., tests/infra_tests/test_validation/, tests/infra_tests/test_rendering/) and use normal package imports for helpers
